behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is module handoff: file discovery + filename parsing + ordering + execution sequencing. A unit test of sort logic alone can miss wiring bugs (e.g., discovered files not passed in sorted order). Integration is sufficient because behavior text is about ordering logic, not real OS/network/subprocess faults. Rejected System: no behavior text about subprocess timing, network conditions, or external contract failures; rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI step.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp migrations directory with out-of-order files (e.g., 010_b.sql, 002_a.sql, 100_c.sql). Run the runner with a test DB adapter/repo that records execution order. Assert executed order is 002, 010, 100 and only .sql files are considered.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Needs persisted state handoff between runs (runner <-> _migrations metadata path), which is beyond a single-function unit test. Integration catches real failure where first run records state and second run must skip execution. Rejected System: behavior text focuses on idempotency across runs, not OS/network/subprocess fault modes. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run runner twice against the same test database/schema with one migration that inserts a marker row. After run 1, assert marker exists and migration recorded. After run 2, assert migration was not executed again (marker count unchanged, metadata still single applied record).

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Rollback semantics and stop-on-failure behavior depend on real DB transaction behavior under an actual failing SQL statement; mocks can hide this (MOCK-HIDE). This is a real infrastructure boundary (database process/connection behavior), so Integration is too low for this failure mode. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI involvement.
behaviors.test_description: Against a real Postgres instance, create migration A with multiple statements where one statement is invalid after an earlier write, and migration B that would make a visible change. Run runner and assert: migration A effects are absent (rolled back), migration B not applied, and metadata reflects no successful apply past failure point.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly requires concurrent separate processes, which is a subprocess/DB-locking boundary. In-process fakes cannot faithfully reproduce race timing and database lock/constraint interaction; mocks may always pass (MOCK-HIDE). Rejected Integration by behavior text: "two instances ... at the same time" disqualifies purely in-process wiring tests. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Start two real runner processes simultaneously against the same Postgres DB containing multiple unapplied migrations. Wait for both to finish. Assert each migration appears exactly once in _migrations and migration side effects exist exactly once (no duplicates, no misses).

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Failure is sequencing across local modules (bootstrap metadata path before apply path) on initial state. This requires wiring-level verification, not just a single unit rule. Rejected System: behavior does not require OS/subprocess/network fault injection; ordering can be verified via integration with test DB/repo state. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Against a fresh test database/schema with no _migrations table, run the runner with one migration file. Assert _migrations table exists and contains the applied entry, and migration apply succeeds without missing-table errors. Optionally verify operation order via query/log hook showing metadata-table creation occurs before first apply insert.
